title: Butterfly Effect
tagline: The flap of a butterfly’s wings can be felt on the other side of the world.
subtitle: A research system for detecting subtle sleep and physiological patterns that may precede future changes in stress, symptoms, and health burden.
lede: Butterfly Effect explores how small changes in sleep and overnight physiology may precede later changes in stress, symptoms, and daily burden. The public surface is designed to explain that idea clearly without blurring the very different layers of evidence underneath it.
hero_note: Nightly within-cohort interpretation, portable event stress, and daily temporal gains already hold. Night transport is still the main bottleneck, though the portable stress D+1 family has now improved under a governed blended contract and remains research-only. Not diagnostic.
meta_description: Butterfly Effect is a research-grade sleep interpretation surface built around nightly routes, daily challenge layers, portable event stress, governance, and evidence-linked reporting.

narrative_blocks.title: What it watches
narrative_blocks.body: Longitudinal sleep timing, continuity, nocturnal cardiovascular load, movement, and repeated self-report signals.

narrative_blocks.title: What it builds
narrative_blocks.body: Nightly interpretation routes, event stress models, daily challenge layers, and portable research families with explicit governance.

narrative_blocks.title: What it returns
narrative_blocks.body: A readable interpretation surface for institutions, participants, and professionals: signal, limits, validation posture, and evidence links kept visible at the same time.

hero_panels.label: Visible cards
hero_panels.detail: night, event, daily, and family surfaces exposed without flattening them into one false product claim

hero_panels.label: Accepted units
hero_panels.detail: cards or families with signals the current evidence can defend inside their intended scope

hero_panels.label: Experimental units
hero_panels.detail: research layers that remain useful but still fall below deployment or transport thresholds

hero_panels.label: Main bottleneck
hero_panels.detail: cross-cohort nightly portability still limits the strongest public claim the project can make

principles.title: Subtle signal, delayed effect
principles.body: The project starts from a simple premise: small variations in sleep and physiology can accumulate into visible changes in stress, symptoms, and day-to-day burden.

principles.title: Interpretation before diagnosis
principles.body: The surface is built to organize and explain signal, not to pretend that current models already justify diagnostic language or universal clinical claims.

principles.title: Evidence before expansion
principles.body: Where signal is strong, the site says so. Where transport fails or outcome equivalence is weak, those limits stay visible instead of being hidden behind presentation.

workstreams.title: Nightly operational routes
workstreams.body: 12 nightly route cards are already materialized, tiered, and governed. This is the operational spine of the project: cohort-matched interpretation, held-out metrics, interval calibration, and deployment status.

workstreams.title: Portable event stress
workstreams.body: 6 event cards keep acute physiological stress separate from nightly sleep claims. This is currently the cleanest open portable layer in the project and should be communicated as its own stack.

workstreams.title: Daily and non-stress layers
workstreams.body: 8 daily challenge cards and 11 non-stress daily cards show where temporal memory helps within cohort, especially for anxiety and depression, without pretending those signals already transport cleanly across cohorts.

workstreams.title: Governance, evidence, and support
workstreams.body: 3 non-stress family cards sit on top of support cohorts, transport probes, route cards, and evidence-linked reporting. The landing is only the visible layer of a deeper validation stack.

evidence_blocks.title: Open cohorts
evidence_blocks.body: The surface rests on open nightly, daily, and event cohorts, plus support cohorts used to keep anxiety, depression, and physical pain anchors scientifically legible.

evidence_blocks.title: Governance
evidence_blocks.body: Every layer preserves its own accepted, experimental, or support status. Nothing rises to the top level just because it looks good in one cohort or one benchmark.

evidence_blocks.title: Evidence linkage
evidence_blocks.body: The public surface sits on top of evidence-linked reporting, route cards, support cards, and transport probes rather than free-form interpretation.

layers.label: Night Layer
layers.key_message: Nightly routes are the core operational layer. They carry held-out signal, interval calibration, governance, and deployment status.

layers.label: Event Layer
layers.key_message: Event models remain separate from nightly interpretation. They are portable for acute physiological stress, not for next-day sleep claims.

layers.label: Daily Challenge Layer
layers.key_message: Daily challenge cohorts show where temporal memory helps within cohort, even when transport still fails across cohorts.

layers.label: Non-Stress Daily Layer
layers.key_message: This layer keeps anxiety, depression, and somatic support visible without overstating clinical portability.

best_card.label: Novartis depression D0 temporal
best_card.note: Novartis daily wearable depression benchmark with temporal memory over shared domains.

layers.label: Non-Stress Portable Families
layers.key_message: Portable non-stress families remain research references. Anxiety is closest to usable; depression and somatic burden are still below transport readiness.

best_card.label: Anxiety
best_card.note: Closest non-stress family to usable portable reference, but still below the route signal floor.

can_defend: Nightly sleep routes are usable within cohort on selected stress, fatigue, and pain-like targets when governance marks them accepted.
Portable event stress is real and reproducible across open challenge cohorts.
Daily temporal memory improves anxiety and depression within cohort.
Anxiety is the strongest non-stress portable family, but it remains research-only.
cannot_defend: Portable depression as a stable cross-cohort family.
Clinical physical pain prediction from the current somatic-burden proxy family.
current_bottlenecks: Nightly cross-cohort transport remains the main bottleneck.
Non-stress families are limited more by transport and outcome equivalence than by infrastructure.
Event and nightly layers must stay separated in communication and governance.
